Q1: I tried to have a routine, honestly I did. I'd set aside time on Tuesdays and Thursdays after my part-time job to study, maybe like two hours each time. But things kept coming up, and with limited resources at home, I couldn't always stick to it. I'd make these visual schedules on paper because that helps me remember things, but following through was another story entirely.
Q2: It varied a lot based on deadlines and honestly just life stuff. When an assignment was due, I'd cram in study time wherever I could find it. Other weeks, especially when nothing was pressing, I'd let it slide more than I should have. My extracurriculars also took up time, so my study pattern was pretty inconsistent even though I wanted it to be regular.
Q3: Usually it was because I had to check if something was due or if there was an announcement. Sometimes my peer support group would message me and remind me to log in, which actually helped a lot. I'd also log in when I felt guilty about not doing enough, though that didn't always lead to productive studying. It was mostly deadline-driven, if I'm being honest.
Q4: For a few weeks in the middle of the semester, logging in became more automatic. My peer support buddy and I had this thing where we'd check in with each other, and that made me log in more regularly. But then life got hectic with work and other commitments, and that routine kind of fell apart. It never really stuck as a permanent habit for me.
Q5: The videos were really helpful for me because I'm a visual learner. I'd watch them and take notes, and things made more sense when I could see diagrams and examples. The discussion posts were also useful because hearing from other students helped me understand concepts I was confused about. I spent quite a bit of time on videos, probably around 90 minutes total, which felt productive.
Q6: I often skipped the longer reading materials and some of the supplementary resources. Honestly, with limited internet access at home sometimes, downloading or streaming extra stuff wasn't always possible. I also tended to ignore practice quizzes until right before exams, which in hindsight was a mistake. If something looked too text-heavy, I'd put it off because visual stuff just works better for my brain.
Q7: If I could see how it connected to an assignment or exam, it felt worth doing. Activities where I could interact with peers also felt valuable because I wasn't just sitting alone struggling. I needed to feel like the time I put in would actually help my grade or my understanding. When something felt disconnected from what I needed to pass, I'd deprioritize it.
Q8: Required activities felt more urgent, like I had to do them no matter what. Optional stuff always got pushed to the back burner because I was already stretched thin. Even when optional activities seemed interesting, I couldn't justify spending time on them when I was behind on required work. It's frustrating because some of those optional resources probably would have helped me understand things better.
Q9: Yeah, definitely. I'd download lecture slides and videos when I had good internet access, then study them offline later. I also used my phone to take pictures of diagrams and review them on the bus to work. A lot of my studying happened with printed notes and handwritten summaries because that's how I retain information best. The LMS wouldn't have seen any of that.
Q10: There were times I'd spend hours going over my handwritten notes or reviewing downloaded materials at home. I'd also study with friends from my peer support group in person, quizzing each other and explaining concepts. None of that showed up anywhere online. Some of my best studying happened away from my computer entirely.
Q11: All the time, actually. I'd watch a video once online, take detailed notes, and then review those notes multiple times offline. With my internet being unreliable sometimes, it made sense to grab things once and work with them later. My notes became my main study tool rather than going back to the LMS repeatedly.
Q12: Around midterms, everything got really hard. I was working more hours because I needed the money, and I was also dealing with stress from other areas of my life. My attendance dropped because I couldn't physically make it to classes, and I fell behind on assignments. Even though my motivation was high, I just didn't have the energy or time to keep up.
Q13: Absolutely. When I was exhausted from work, I'd log into the LMS but just stare at the screen without really absorbing anything. Competing responsibilities meant I had to choose between studying and other important things like earning money. The confusion from missing classes made the LMS content harder to understand, which made me less likely to engage deeply with it.
Q14: Yeah, more often than I'd like to admit. I'd open videos or readings with good intentions but find myself zoning out or getting distracted. Sometimes I was so tired that the words just didn't register. I'd technically be logged in and looking at materials, but my brain wasn't actually processing anything meaningful.
Q15: I postponed assignments a lot, even when I knew they were important. Part of it was feeling overwhelmed and not knowing where to start. Part of it was low resilience—when things got hard, I'd avoid them instead of pushing through. I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow, and then tomorrow I'd be even more behind.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me to actually submit work. I'd have this burst of activity right before something was due, cramming everything in at the last minute. Without a deadline looming, I'd let things slide. It wasn't healthy, but that pressure was what motivated me to actually complete things.
Q17: When I got feedback, it did affect me, but not always in a helpful way. Good feedback motivated me to try harder on the next thing. But when feedback was critical, it sometimes made me feel like there was no point in trying. My low resilience meant I took negative feedback pretty hard and sometimes avoided checking my grades.
Q18: The course structure was okay, but it assumed you could keep up with a regular pace. When I fell behind, it was hard to catch up because everything built on previous material. The peer support intervention helped because it gave me some structure and accountability outside of just deadlines. But overall, the course moved faster than I could manage with everything else going on.
Q19: Having videos made engagement easier because I could learn visually. But the heavy reading requirements made it harder since that's not my strongest learning style. The discussion forums were helpful when peers were active, but sometimes posts went unanswered which was discouraging. More flexibility with deadlines would have made a huge difference for someone in my situation.
Q20: I think that could be someone like me, honestly. You log in a lot trying to figure things out, watching videos, reading discussions, but then when it comes to actually completing and submitting work, you run out of time or energy. High activity doesn't mean you're successfully completing things—it might mean you're struggling and searching for help without finding it.
Q21: That makes sense if someone studies a lot offline or already has background knowledge. I wasn't really in that position, but I can see how someone could download everything once, study effectively on their own, and not need to log in much. The LMS activity doesn't capture what happens in your notebook or in your head.
Q22: Course logs can show when you logged in and what you clicked on, but they can't show if you actually understood anything. They miss offline studying, mental engagement, and the quality of your learning. They also can't capture what's going on in your life that affects your ability to engage. It's like measuring only part of the picture.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about learning the material and trying to understand it, not just going through the motions. It's about being mentally present, asking questions, and connecting with the content. For me, it also involves connecting with peers and feeling like I'm part of a learning community. You can be logged in without being engaged, and you can be deeply engaged without being online.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how hard I was actually trying despite my circumstances. It wouldn't show the hours I spent with handwritten notes or studying with my peer support group in person. It also wouldn't show the stress I was under from work and limited resources, or how much I wanted to succeed even though my grades didn't reflect that. My effort and intention were way higher than my results suggest.
